title: Pet-Friendly Summer Travel Guide: Road Trips, Hotels & National Parks That Welcome Dogs
excerpt: Over 70% of US travelers with pets plan to bring them on summer trips this year. Here’s what you need to pack, book, and budget about pet-friendly hotels, airline policies, national park rules, and road trip essentials for traveling with your dog in summer 2026.
description: A complete guide to traveling with pets in summer 2026 — pet-friendly hotels, airline pet policies, national park rules, road trip essentials, and budget tips for traveling with dogs.

placeholderSections.heading: Why Pet-Friendly Travel Is Booming in 2026
placeholderSections.body: Pet-friendly travel is one of the fastest-growing segments of the summer 2026 tourism industry. According to the American Pet Products Association (APPA), 70% of U.S. households — roughly 90 million homes — own a pet, and the American Hotel & Lodging Association (AHLA) reports that 74% of hotel properties now allow pets, up from 65% in 2020. The trend accelerated during the pandemic as pet adoption surged, and summer 2026 marks the first peak travel season where pet-friendly is a default expectation rather than a niche offering. Booking.com's 2026 Travel Predictions survey found that 53% of pet owners plan to bring their pet on at least one trip this summer. This shift has major implications for budget travelers: pet boarding costs $35–$60 per night in most cities, so bringing your pet can save $245–$420 on a week-long trip. But it also means you need to plan ahead — pet-friendly hotel rooms book faster and cost 10–20% more than standard rooms. For a broader look at how summer 2026 travel trends are reshaping the market, see our [summer travel crisis explainer](/articles/2026-summer-travel-crisis-explained). The American Pet Products Association reports that annual pet spending in the U.S. has exceeded $150 billion in 2026, with travel-related pet expenses growing faster than any other category (akc.org, June 2026).

placeholderSections.heading: Pet-Friendly Hotels: Which Chains Welcome Dogs Best
placeholderSections.body: Not all pet-friendly hotels are created equal. Here is how the major chains compare for summer 2026. Kimpton Hotels: the gold standard — no pet fees, no weight limits, and no breed restrictions. All Kimpton properties welcome pets with amenities like pet beds, bowls, and treats at check-in. Room rates $180–$350/night. La Quinta by Wyndham: the budget-friendly winner — no pet fees at most locations, dogs under 50 lbs welcome, and rates average $80–$130/night. The catch: some franchise locations have their own policies, so call ahead to confirm. Motel 6: no pet fees at all locations, any size dog, and rates from $60–$100/night. Perfect for road trip pit stops. Best Western: $20–$30/night pet fee per pet, up to two dogs, weight limit varies by location (typically 50–80 lbs). Rates $80–$150/night. Marriott brands: vary widely — Residence Inn and Element are pet-friendly with a $75–$150 non-refundable fee per stay, while other Marriott brands charge $50–$100 per night. Hilton brands: Embassy Suites and Homewood Suites are pet-friendly with a $50–$75 one-time fee. The key decision factor: total cost with pet fees. A Motel 6 or La Quinta with no pet fee is often cheaper than a comparable room with a $30/night pet fee, especially on multi-night stays. For families traveling with pets, combining a pet-friendly hotel with a road trip can dramatically reduce your overall trip cost — read our [budget road trip planning guide](/articles/budget-road-trip-planning-guide) for the full strategy.

placeholderSections.heading: Flying with Pets in Summer 2026: Airline Policies Compared
placeholderSections.body: Airline pet policies have tightened in the post-Spirit era, with most carriers raising fees and restricting cabin pet access during peak heat months. Here is the summer 2026 scenery. In-cabin pets (under 20 lbs with carrier): Delta charges $95 each way, United $125, American $125, Southwest $95, Alaska $100, Frontier $99, JetBlue $125. All limit pets to the cabin floor under the seat in front of you — carriers must be soft-sided and fit under the seat (typically 18x11x11 inches or smaller). Cargo pets (larger dogs): only Alaska and United still offer temperature-controlled cargo pet transport during summer months. Delta, American, and Southwest have suspended pet cargo service from June through September due to heat-safety concerns. The cost: $200–$300 each way. The big change in 2026: several airlines now require a pet health certificate signed by a veterinarian within 10 days of travel — even for in-cabin pets. The certificate costs $50–$150 from your vet. Budget tip: book early — most airlines limit in-cabin pets to 4–6 per flight, and summer flights fill their pet quota weeks in advance. For travelers who can drive instead, the savings add up quickly. A family flying with one pet pays $190–$250 roundtrip in pet fees alone, plus the health certificate. Driving eliminates that entirely. Check our [road trip vs flying cost comparison](/articles/road-trip-vs-flying-cost-comparison) for the full cost breakdown.

placeholderSections.heading: National Parks: Where Dogs Are Welcome (and Where They Aren't)
placeholderSections.body: National park pet policies are a perennial source of confusion for travelers. The short version: dogs are allowed in developed areas of most national parks (campgrounds, parking lots, paved roads, and some paved trails), but they are generally not allowed on hiking trails, in wilderness areas, or in buildings. The America the Beautiful Pass ($80) covers entry for a vehicle and its occupants — it does not add any pet-specific benefits. The most dog-friendly national parks include: Shenandoah (dogs allowed on all 500+ miles of trails, including the Appalachian Trail), Acadia (100+ miles of pet-friendly carriage roads and several trails), Petrified Forest (dogs allowed on all trails), and Grand Canyon's South Rim (dogs on paved rim trail only). Parks with strict limits: Yosemite (dogs only on paved trails and the paved paths in Yosemite Valley), Zion (dogs limited to the Pa'rus Trail — the only pet-friendly trail in the park), and Yellowstone (dogs only in developed areas, parking lots, and campgrounds — not on any boardwalks or trails). The 2026 change that matters: several parks — including Rocky Mountain, Glacier, and Great Smoky Mountains — have expanded their BARK Ranger programs (pet-friendly ranger walks and education sessions), but none have loosened trail restrictions. If hiking with your dog is important, state parks and national forests are a better bet: most state parks allow dogs on trails, and national forests have no dog restrictions outside of designated wilderness areas. For more on park reservation changes this year, see our [national parks 2026 guide](/articles/national-parks-2026-summer-guide).

placeholderSections.heading: Road Trip Essentials for Traveling with a Dog
placeholderSections.body: A successful pet road trip comes down to preparation. Here is the checklist for summer 2026. Safety: a crash-tested dog harness (SleepyPod Clickit or Ruffwear Load Up, $65–$90) or a secured travel crate is essential — an unsecured 50-lb dog becomes a 2,500-lb projectile in a 50 MPH crash. Never let your dog ride with its head out the window (debris injuries are common). Comfort: bring a familiar bed or blanket, a collapsible water bowl ($10), and enough of your dog's regular food for the entire trip plus 2 extra days (switching food causes digestive issues). Hydration breaks every 2–3 hours — use the BringFido app to find pet-friendly rest stops. Temperature: never leave a dog alone in a parked car, even for just a minute. On a 75°F day, a car's interior reaches 100°F in 20 minutes. Many states have Good Samaritan laws allowing bystanders to break car windows to rescue a distressed pet. Documentation: carry proof of current vaccinations (especially rabies) and a recent photo of your dog in case it gets lost. Microchip registration should be up to date. Health note: if your dog gets carsick or anxious, talk to your vet about options — Dramamine (dimenhydrinate) is safe for dogs at 2–4 mg per pound, given 30–60 minutes before travel. For a deeper dive on packing strategies that apply to pet travel too, check our [packing hacks article](/articles/packing-hacks-to-save-on-baggage-fees).

placeholderSections.heading: Budget-Friendly Pet Travel: How to Save
placeholderSections.body: Traveling with a pet doesn't have to blow your budget. Here are real cost-saving strategies for summer 2026. (1) Choose no-pet-fee hotels: Motel 6, La Quinta (most locations), and Kimpton (no fees, though the room rate is higher) eliminate the $20–$50/night pet surcharge. A week at Motel 6 with a pet costs $420–$700 total versus $700–$1,400 at a pet-friendly Marriott with fees. (2) Camp instead: most KOA campgrounds and state park campgrounds charge no pet fees for tent and RV sites. A KOA cabin ($60–$100/night) with a pet is often cheaper than a budget motel with pet fees. (3) Skip airline pet fees by driving: as calculated above, a roundtrip pet flight fee of $190–$250 plus a $50–$150 health certificate adds up quickly. For trips under 500 miles, driving with a pet is almost always cheaper. (4) Use the BringFido app (bringfido.com) to filter hotels by pet fee — it shows the total cost with fees included, preventing surprise charges at checkout. (5) Pack pet supplies from home: buying food, bowls, leashes, and waste bags at your destination costs 20–40% more than bringing them from home. The bottom line: with smart choices, traveling with a pet adds 10–20% to your total trip cost instead of the 30–50% surge that unprepared travelers experience. For additional budget-saving travel strategies, see our [10 cheapest US destinations guide](/articles/10-cheapest-us-destinations-for-summer-2026) — several of those destinations (Chattanooga, Asheville, Kansas City) are notably pet-friendly with multiple no-fee hotel options.
